ITEM 1.    Business
OVERVIEW
Visa is one of the world's leaders in digital payments. Our purpose is to uplift everyone, everywhere by being the best way to pay and be paid. We facilitate global commerce and money movement across more than 200 countries and territories among a global set of consumers, merchants, financial institutions and government entities through innovative technologies.
Since Visa's early days in 1958, we have been in the business of facilitating payments between consumers and businesses. As a trusted engine of commerce and with new ways to pay, we are working to provide payment solutions for everyone, everywhere. We are focused on extending, enhancing and investing in our proprietary network, VisaNet, to offer a single connection point for facilitating payment transactions to multiple endpoints through various form factors. Through our network, we offer products, solutions and services that facilitate secure, reliable and efficient money movement for participants in the ecosystem.
- We facilitate secure, reliable and efficient money movement among consumers, issuing and acquiring financial institutions, and merchants. We have traditionally referred to this as the "four-party" model. Please see Our Core Business discussion below. As the payments ecosystem continues to evolve, we have broadened this model to include digital banks, digital wallets and a range of financial technology companies (fintechs), governments and non-governmental organizations (NGOs). We provide transaction processing services (primarily authorization, clearing and settlement) to our financial institution and merchant clients through VisaNet, our advanced transaction processing network. During fiscal year 2022, we saw 258 billio
…[truncated 20268 chars]…
 added value to our clients and their customers, financial institutions, merchants and partners through compelling brand expressions, a wide range of products and services as well as innovative brand and marketing efforts. In line with our commitment to an expansive and diverse range of partnerships for the benefit of our stakeholders, Visa is the only brand in the world that is a top sponsor of FIFA, the
11
Table of Contents
Olympic Games, and the NFL and is one of the most active sponsors of women's football around the world.
Talent
Attracting, developing and advancing the best talent globally is critical to our continued success. This year we grew our total workforce from approximately 21,500 in fiscal year 2021 to approximately 26,500 employees in fiscal year 2022, an increase of 23 percent year over year. Voluntary workforce turnover (rolling 12-month attrition) was 12.1 percent as of September 30, 2022. Visa employees are located in more than 80 countries and territories, with 54 percent located outside the U.S. At the end of fiscal year 2022, Visa's global workforce was 58 percent men and 42 percent women, and women represented 36 percent of Visa's leadership (defined as vice president level and above). In the U.S., ethnicity of our workforce was 41 percent Asian, 8 percent Black, 12 percent Hispanic, 3 percent Other and 36 percent White. For our U.S. leadership, the breakdown was 18 percent Asian, 7 percent Black, 13 percent Hispanic, 2 percent Other and 60 percent White.
Given Visa's ambitious growth agenda, it is important to enable our employees to achieve their individual performance goals while also supporting personal career interests. This year we introduced several changes to career growth and planning at Visa, including new growth paths and tools that take into consideration the unique professional backgrounds, skills, accomplishments, and future performance goals of our employees. These tools support meaningful dialogue about performance and help drive development, retention and growth of top talent in a highly competitive talent market.
We have an unwavering commitment to valuing the unique identities of our employees and their contributions to Visa. In 2020, we established the Stand Together initiative in support of social justice and racial equality in the U.S. focused on our people, our community and our company. We are proud of the progress we have made. Our partnership with the Thurgood Marshall College Fund for the Visa Black Scholars and Jobs Program resulted in Visa's inaugural class of 51 scholars participating in year-round programs and training aimed at developing their professional and technical skills this past year. We also welcomed the second cohort of 75 scholars this fall. Upon graduation, all scholars who have met their commitments will be offered a full-time job with Visa.
We continue to increase the number of underrepresented employees in the U.S. We are committed to recruiting and retaining diverse talent through employee development programs aimed at advancing their careers at Visa. As a company, we continue to partner with historically Black colleges and a generally more diverse set of universities to further develop our talent pipeline. Visa is committed to pay equity, regardless of gender or race/ethnicity, and conducts pay equity analyses on an annual basis. More details regarding our human capital management, as well as enhanced workforce disclosures that include our 2021 Consolidated EEO-1 Report and our 2021 Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Report, can be found on our website at visa.com/esg. See Available Information below.
For additional information, please see the section titled "Talent and Human Capital Management" in Visa's 2022 Proxy Statement.
FINTECH AND DIGITAL PARTNERSHIPS
Fintechs are key enablers of new payment experiences and new flows. Our work with fintechs has opened new points of acceptance, extended credit at the point of sale, made cross-border money flows more efficient, moved B2B spend onto Visa's network, expedited payroll and provided digital wallet customers access to our services.
To better serve fintechs, Visa has a suite of streamlined commercial programs and digital onboarding tools. Our Fintech Fast Track program enables qualifying fintechs to quickly launch and scale their programs. The program has welcomed hundreds of fintechs who are actively engaged in the program.
With our startup engagement programs, the Visa Everywhere Initiative and the Inclusive Fintech 50, early-stage companies can build payment solutions based on our capabilities. With the global Visa Fintech Partner Connect program, we are helping our clients tap into innovations emerging from the fintech community. The program is designed to help financial institutions quickly connect with a curated set of technology providers, helping Visa's issuing partners create digital-first experiences.
MERGERS AND ACQUISITIONS, JOINT VENTURES AND STRATEGIC INVESTMENTS
Visa continually explores opportunities to augment our capabilities and provide meaningful value to our clients. Mergers and acquisitions, joint ventures and strategic investments complement our internal development and

enhance our partnerships to align with Visa's priorities. Visa applies a rigorous business analysis to our acquisitions, joint ventures and investments to ensure they will differentiate our network, provide value added services and accelerate growth.
In fiscal year 2022, we acquired The Currency Cloud Group Limited (Currencycloud), a global platform that enables financial institutions and fintechs to provide innovative, cross-border foreign exchange solutions. We also acquired Tink, an open banking platform that enables financial institutions, fintechs and merchants to build financial products and services and move money.
ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL AND GOVERNANCE
As a trusted brand, Visa has an opportunity and responsibility to contribute to a more inclusive, equitable and sustainable world. We believe that economies that include everyone everywhere, uplift everyone everywhere. As we build business resilience and long-term value, we are committed to managing the risks and opportunities that arise from ESG issues. We are focused on empowering people and economies; securing commerce and protecting customers; investing in our workforce; protecting the planet; and operating responsibly. Our 2021 ESG report, as well as other ESG-related resources are available on our website at visa.com/esg. See Available Information below.
INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY
We own and manage the Visa brand, which stands for acceptance, security, convenience, speed and reliability. Our portfolio of Visa-owned trademarks is important to our business. Generally, trademark registrations are valid indefinitely as long as they are in use and/or maintained. We give our clients access to these assets through agreements with our issuers and acquirers, which authorize the use of our trademarks in connection with their participation in our payments network. Additionally, we own a number of patents and patent applications related to our business and continue to pursue patents in emerging technologies that may have applications in our business. We rely on a combination of patent, trademark, copyright and trade secret laws in the U.S. and other jurisdictions, as well as confidentiality procedures and contractual provisions, to protect our proprietary technology.
COMPETITION
The global payments industry continues to undergo dynamic change. Existing and emerging competitors compete with Visa's network and payment solutions for consumers and for participation by financial institutions and merchants. Technology and innovation are shifting consumer habits and driving growth opportunities in ecommerce, mobile payments, blockchain technology and digital currencies. V.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Report: Visa Inc. (V) as of 2023-04-01**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
*   **Revenue:** Demonstrated consistent quarterly growth. Latest quarter (2022-12-31) revenue was $7.94B, a +12.4% YoY increase from $7.06B. The TTM revenue sum is $30.19B.
*   **Earnings & Margins:** Net income in the latest quarter was $4.18B. TTM net income is $15.18B. Operating margin is high and stable, with the latest quarter at 64.1% ($5.09B operating income / $7.94B revenue). Net margin is consistently ~50% (latest: 0.503).
*   **Cash-Flow:** Data is unavailable for all reported periods.
*   **Balance Sheet:** Total equity grew from $36.19B (2021-12-31) to $36.92B (2022-12-31). Total debt was $20.92B as of 2021-12-31; later debt figures are unavailable. The debt-to-equity ratio (using 2021-12-31 debt and 2022-12-31 equity) is approximately 0.57.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** High and stable operating margins (consistently >60%) and net margins (~50%) suggest strong, durable earnings power. The trajectory shows steady growth in both revenue and net income.
*   **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** The equity base is growing. The debt-to-equity ratio of ~0.57 indicates moderate leverage. However, a full assessment of resilience is limited by the absence of recent debt and cash flow data.

**3. Valuation versus Company's Own Supplied History**
*   Key valuation multiples (P/E, EV/EBIT, FCF Yield, P/B) are unavailable in the supplied data.
*   The available profitability metrics (net margin, ROE) have been stable over the past year. Net margin ranged from 0.503 to 0.52, and ROE ranged from 0.363 to 0.42. The latest ROE is 0.411.

**4. Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context**
*   **Price Level:** The latest close (2023-03-31) is $225.46, near the 52-week high of $250.58 and well above the 52-week low of $174.60.
*   **Returns:** The stock returned +8.4% over the last quarter (from $208.06) but only +0.7% over the last year (from $223.95). The quarterly path shows volatility, including a -12.1% drop in Q2 2022 and a +15.6% gain in Q4 2022.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
*   **Upside:** Consistent double-digit YoY revenue growth (+12.4%) and industry-leading operating margins (~64%) provide a strong fundamental foundation. The TTM operating income of $19.13B demonstrates significant scale.
*   **Downside:** The stock's annual return is flat (+0.7%) despite strong earnings growth, suggesting the market may have already priced in the positive trajectory. The absence of cash flow data and recent debt figures creates a gap in assessing true financial flexibility. The price is near its 52-week high, potentially limiting near-term upside.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
The fundamental trajectory is positive, characterized by robust revenue growth and elite profitability margins. However, the quantitative evidence for valuation is incomplete, as critical multiples (P/E, EV/EBIT) are unavailable. The stock's price has shown recent quarterly strength but is flat over a year, indicating the strong fundamentals may be reflected in the price. The risk/reward is neutral to slightly positive based on the visible growth and margin strength, but a definitive conclusion is hampered by the lack of valuation and cash flow data.
V.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Analysis: Visa (V)
**Evidence Cutoff:** 2023-03-30
**Assessment Date:** 2023-04-01

**1. Business/Franchise Read (from 10-K filing, 2022-11-16)**
Visa is a global leader in digital payments, operating a proprietary network (VisaNet) to facilitate transactions among consumers, merchants, financial institutions, and governments. Its core business is built on a "four-party" model, with key metrics including processing 193 billion transactions in fiscal year 2022 and having 4.1 billion credentials in circulation globally. The company's strategy is to accelerate growth in three areas: consumer payments (shifting cash/checks to digital), new flows (e.g., Visa Direct, B2B), and value-added services (risk, advisory, issuer processing). Visa emphasizes its evolution into a "network of networks" to enable money movement across all available networks and form factors.

**2. Recent Developments (from supplied news and web context)**
*   **Legal/Regulatory:** An appeals court upheld a $5.6 billion antitrust settlement with retailers regarding interchange fees on **2023-03-15**. Separately, new competition lawsuits were reported in the UK on **2023-03-14**.
*   **Market Sentiment & Outlook:** Visa was highlighted as a "trending stock" and bullish conviction pick on **2023-03-01** and **2023-03-30**, respectively. Earnings estimates for the upcoming quarter (as of **2023-03-14**) projected year-over-year revenue growth of approximately 7.7%.
*   **Strategic Catalysts:** A **2023-03-06** analysis noted Visa's recovery from COVID-19 lows and ongoing opportunity in the secular shift from cash to digital payments, especially in emerging markets.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Regulatory & Legal Overhang.** The **2023-03-15** settlement of the massive interchange fee lawsuit and the **2023-03-14** report of fresh UK lawsuits confirm ongoing and material legal exposure related to its core fee structure.
*   **Offset: Structural Growth Position.** The 10-K (2022-11-16) and subsequent commentary (**2023-03-06**) consistently frame the long-term, secular shift from cash to digital payments as a primary growth driver, which benefits Visa's network role.
*   **Offset: Network Dominance and Innovation.** Evidence from the 10-K highlights Visa's massive scale (4.1B credentials, 80M+ merchant locations), investments in security (tokenization), and expansion into new flows (Visa Direct, B2B Connect), providing competitive moats.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral to Positive.** The franchise demonstrates clear strength through its dominant global network position and exposure to long-term digital payment tailwinds. Recent narratives highlight sustained growth expectations. However, the material and confirmed legal risks surrounding its core interchange fee model present a persistent and significant qualitative headwind that tempers the outlook.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Cash-Flow: Data is unavailable for all reported periods' and later says 'The absence of cash flow data and recent debt figures creates a gap.' The raw evidence confirms cash flow data is indeed absent for all periods, so this is accurate. However, the quant report also states 'The debt-to-equity ratio (using 2021-12-31 debt and 2022-12-31 equity) is approximately 0.57.' The raw evidence shows total_debt=20.92B only for 2021-12-31 and earlier periods, with no debt figures for 2022. The report correctly flags this limitation but still computes a ratio mixing mismatched dates without clearly labeling it as a stale/cross-period estimate. This is a minor presentation issue but could mislead on balance-sheet currentness.
corrections.required_action: No action required for PM; the report does flag the data gap. Minor note: the D/E ratio uses stale debt (2021-12-31) with current equity (2022-12-31), so the ~0.57 figure should be treated as an upper-bound estimate at best, since 2022 debt may have changed.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'Earnings estimates for the upcoming quarter (as of 2023-03-14) projected year-over-year revenue growth of approximately 7.7%.' The raw qualitative evidence from the 2023-03-14 Yahoo Finance article states: 'the Zacks Consensus Estimate for revenue is projecting net sales of $7.74 billion, up 7.72% from the year-ago period.' This is consistent. However, the qual report also references 'bullish conviction pick on 2023-03-30' — the raw evidence for that date is a Zacks article titled '3 Dow Stocks That Billionaires Are Buying Hand Over Fist' which mentions V among Dow components bought by billionaires. The characterization as a 'bullish conviction pick' is a reasonable inference but slightly overstates the specificity of the source.
corrections.required_action: No action required; characterization is directionally correct.

corrections.issue: The qual report's Section 1 cites 'processing 193 billion transactions in fiscal year 2022' and '4.1 billion credentials in circulation globally.' The raw 10-K evidence confirms both figures. However, the 10-K also states '258 billion payments and cash transactions with Visa's brand' of which 193 billion were processed by Visa — the report only mentions the 193B processed figure, omitting the larger 258B total branded figure. This is a minor omission that slightly understates Visa's total transaction reach.
corrections.required_action: No action required; the 193B processed figure is the more operationally relevant metric.

unsupported_claims: : The quant report's D/E ratio of ~0.57 uses 2021-12-31 debt ($20.92B) paired with 2022-12-31 equity ($36.92B). The raw evidence provides no total_debt figure for any 2022 period, making this a cross-period calculation. While the report does note the data limitation, the ratio is presented as a standalone metric without clear labeling that it may not reflect the current leverage position.
verified_points

Latest 10-K business section (newest first):
  filing_date=2022-08-05 section=business period_end=2022-06-30
Item 1.  Business.
The Procter & Gamble Company (the Company) is focused on providing branded products of superior quality and value to improve the lives of the world's consumers, now and for generations to come.  The Company was incorporated in Ohio in 1905, having first been established as a New Jersey corporation in 1890, and was built from a business founded in Cincinnati in 1837 by William Procter and James Gamble.  Today, our products are sold in approximately 180 countries and territories.
Additional information required by this item is incorporated herein by reference to Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A); and Notes 1 and 2 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.  Unless the context indicates otherwise, the terms the "Company," "P&G," "we," "our" or "us" as used herein refer to The Procter & Gamble Company (the registrant) and its subsidiaries.
Throughout this Form 10-K, we incorporate by reference information from other documents filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).
The Company's Annual Report on Form 10-K, quarterly reports on Form 10-Q and current reports on Form 8-K, and amendments thereto, are filed electronically with the SEC.  The SEC maintains an internet site that contains these reports at: www.sec.gov.  Reports can also be accessed through links from our website at: www.pginvestor.com.  P&G includes the website link solely as a textual reference.  The information contained on our website is not incorporated by reference into this report.
Copies of these reports are also available, without charge, by contacting EQ Shareowner Services, 1100 Centre Pointe Curve, Suite 101, Mendota, MN 55120-4100.
Financial Information about Segments
Information about our reportable segments can be found in the MD&A and Note 2 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.
Narrative Description of Business
Business Model
.  Our business model relies on the continued growth and success of existing brands and products, as well as the creation of new innovative products and brands.  The markets and industry segments in which we offer our products are highly competitive.  Our products are sold in approximately 180 countries and territories through numerous channels as well as direct-to-consumer.  Our growth strategy is to deliver meaningful and noticeable superiority across five key vectors of our consumer proposition - product performance, packaging, brand communication, retail execution and consumer and customer value.  We use our research and development (R&D) and consumer insights to provide superior products and packaging.  We utilize our marketing and online presence to deliver superior brand messaging to our co
…[truncated 2138 chars]…
 these patents or licenses cover significant product formulation and processes used to manufacture our products.  The trademarks are important to the overall marketing and branding of our products.  All major trademarks in each business are registered.  In part, our success can be attributed to the existence and continued protection of these trademarks, patents and licenses.
Competitive Condition
.  The markets in which our products are sold are highly competitive.  Our products compete against similar products of many large and small companies, including well-known global competitors.  In many of the markets and industry segments in which we sell our products, we compete against other branded products as well as retailers' private-label brands.  We are well positioned in the industry segments and markets in which we operate, often holding a leadership or significant market share position.  We support our products with advertising, promotions and other marketing vehicles to build awareness
2        The Procter & Gamble Company
and trial of our brands and products in conjunction with our sales force.  We believe this combination provides the most efficient method of marketing for these types of products.  Product quality, performance, value and packaging are also important differentiating factors.
Government Regulation
.  Our Company is subject to a wide variety of laws and regulations across the countries in which we do business.  In the United States, many of our products and manufacturing operations are subject to one or more federal or state regulatory agencies, including the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and the Consumer Product Safety Commission (CPSC).  We are also subject to anti-corruption laws and regulations, such as the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, and antitrust and competition laws and regulations that govern our dealings with suppliers, customers, competitors and government officials.
In addition, many foreign jurisdictions in which we do business have regulations and regulatory bodies that govern similar aspects of our operations and products, in some cases to an even more significant degree.  We are also subject to expanding laws and regulations related to environmental protection and other sustainability-related matters, non-financial reporting and diligence, labor and employment, trade, taxation and data privacy and protection, including the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and similar regulations in states within the United States and in countries around the world.  For additional information on the potential impacts of global legal and regulatory requirements on our business, see "Item 1A. Risk Factors" herein.
The Company has in place compliance programs and internal and external experts to help guide our business in complying with these and other existing laws and regulations that apply to us around the globe; and we have made, and plan to continue making, necessary expenditures for compliance with these laws and regulations.  We also expect that our many suppliers, consultants and other third parties working on our behalf share our commitment to compliance, and we have policies and procedures in place to manage these relationships, though they inherently involve a lesser degree of control over operations and governance.  We do not expect that the Company's expenditures for compliance with current government regulations, including current environmental regulations, will have a material effect on our total capital expenditures, earnings or competitive position in fiscal year 2023 as compared to prior periods.
Human Capital
.  Our employees are a key source of competitive advantage.  Their actions, guided by our Purpose, Values and Principles (PVPs), are critical to the long-term success of our business.  We aim to retain our talented employees by offering competitive compensation and benefits, strong career development and a respectful and inclusive culture that provides equal opportunity for all.
Our Board of Directors, through the Compensation and Leadership Development Committee (C&LD Committee), provides oversight of the Company's policies and strategies relating to talent including diversity, equality and inclusion as well as the Company's compensation principles and practices.  The C&LD Committee also evaluates and approves the Company's compensation plans, policies and programs applicable to our senior executives.
Employees
As of June 30, 2022, the Company had approximately 106,000 employees, an increase of five percent versus the prior year due primarily to business growth.  The total number of employees is an estimate of total Company employees excluding interns, co-ops, contractors and employees of joint ventures.  49% of our employees are in manufacturing roles and 26% of our employees are located in the United States.  41% of our global employees are women.  As of June 30, 2022, 28% of our U.S. employees identify as multicultural.
Training and Development
We focus on attracting, developing and retaining skilled and diverse talent, both from universities and the broader market.  We recruit from among the best universities across markets in which we compete and are generally able to select from the top talent.  We focus on developing our employees by providing a variety of job experiences, training programs and skill development opportunities.  Given our develop-from-within model for staffing most of our senior leadership positions, it is particularly important for us to ensure holistic growth and full engagement of our employees.
Diversity, Equality and Inclusion
As a consumer products company, we believe that it is important for our workforce to reflect the diversity of our consumers worldwide.  We also seek to foster an inclusive work environment where each individual can bring their authentic self, which helps drive innovation and enables us to better serve our consumers.  We aspire to achieve equal gender representation globally and at key management and leadership levels.  Within the U.S. workforce, our aspiration is to achieve 40% multicultural representation overall as well as at management and leadership levels.
Compensation and Benefits
Our compensation plans are based on the principles of paying for performance, paying competitively versus peer companies that we compete with for talent and in the marketplace and focusing on long-term success through a combination of short-term and long-term incentive programs.  We also offer competitive benefit programs, including retirement plans and health insurance in line with local country practices with flexibility to accommodate the needs of a diverse workforce.
Sustainability
.  Environmental sustainability is a key focus area and integrated into P&G's business strategies.  The Company has declared its focus on developing irresistibly superior products and packages that are sustainable.  The Company announced an ambition to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, purchase renewable electricity for our operations, reduce our use of virgin petroleum-based plastic in our packaging, increase the recyclability or reusability of our packaging and increase responsible sourcing of key forest-based commodities such as wood pulp and palm oil.
The Procter & Gamble Company        3
Additional detailed information on our sustainability efforts including our TCFD (Task Force on Climate-Related Financial Disclosures), SASB (Sustainability Accounting Standards Board) and CDP (Carbon Disclosure Project) reports can be found on our website at https://pginvestor.com/esg.  References to our sustainability reports and website are for informational purposes only and neither the sustainability reports nor the other information on our website is incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K.
PG.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Report: PG (Procter & Gamble)**
**Decision Date: 2023-04-01**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
*   **Revenue & Earnings:** Latest quarterly (2022-12-31) revenue was $20.77B, with diluted EPS of $1.59. The quarterly trajectory shows revenue ranging from $18.11B (2021-03-31) to $20.95B (2021-12-31). Year-over-year, revenue declined -0.9% and EPS declined -4.2%. The trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue was $80.28B with net income of $14.35B.
*   **Margins:** Latest gross margin was 46.6%, operating margin 21.6%, and net margin 17.9%. These margins have been relatively stable, with net margin ranging from 0.179 to 0.185 over the past year.
*   **Cash-Flow:** Cash flow data is unavailable for all reported periods.
*   **Balance Sheet:** Total assets were $117.72B, total liabilities $72.99B, and total equity $44.73B. Equity has fluctuated between $44.33B and $46.92B over the last eight quarters.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** Net margin stability (0.179-0.185) suggests consistent operational control. However, the YoY decline in EPS (-4.2%) and net income (-6.7%) indicates recent earnings pressure. The absence of cash flow data prevents assessment of cash conversion.
*   **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** The debt-to-equity ratio (total liabilities / total equity) was 1.63 ($72.99B / $44.73B). Total equity has decreased from $46.92B (2021-03-31) to $44.73B (2022-12-31), a decline of 4.7%.

**3. Valuation versus Supplied History**
*   The latest P/E (diluted) was 25.72, within its 1-year range of 21.3 to 28.3. The P/B ratio was 8.25, within its 1-year range of 6.7 to 9.1. The earnings yield was 3.9%.
*   Compared to its own history over the past year, the current P/E is near the midpoint of its range, not at a significant discount. The P/B is also in the upper half of its recent range.

**4. Price Level and Return Context versus Fundamentals**
*   The latest price (2023-03-31) was $148.69. The quarterly return (from 2022-12-29) was -2.6%, and the annual return (from 2022-03-30) was -3.7%.
*   The stock's 52-week range was $122.18 to $164.90. The recent price decline (-2.6% quarterly) occurred alongside a period of flat-to-negative YoY earnings growth (-4.2% EPS). The price is below its 52-week high but well above its low.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
*   **Upside:** Stable, high gross margins (46.6%) and consistent net margins (17.9%) demonstrate durable pricing power and cost control. The TTM operating income of $17.35B is substantial.
*   **Downside:** The YoY decline in key earnings metrics (EPS -4.2%, net income -6.7%) is a negative trend. The balance sheet shows a gradual erosion of total equity. The lack of cash flow data is a significant analytical gap.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
Based on the point-in-time numbers, PG presents a profile of operational stability (high, stable margins) but with recent negative earnings momentum and a balance sheet showing slight equity erosion. Valuation metrics are not at historical lows, trading near the middle of their one-year ranges. The price has underperformed over the past year (-3.7%) in line with the earnings decline. The risk is continued earnings pressure; the reward hinges on a stabilization of earnings growth. The absence of cash flow data limits a full assessment of financial health.
PG.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: Procter & Gamble (PG)**
**Decision Date: 2023-04-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
Based on the 2022 10-K filing (period ending 2022-06-30), Procter & Gamble is a global consumer goods company focused on branded products of superior quality and value. Its business model is built on growth through existing brands and innovation, competing in highly competitive markets across approximately 180 countries. The company's strategy emphasizes "meaningful and noticeable superiority" across five vectors: product performance, packaging, brand communication, retail execution, and consumer/customer value. It holds leadership or significant market share in its segments. The company is heavily reliant on key retail customers, with Walmart accounting for ~15% of total sales. It is subject to extensive global government regulation and environmental/sustainability laws.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Guidance & Outlook (2023-03-16, 2023-03-19):** Management in January 2023 raised its fiscal year 2023 organic sales growth forecast to a range of 4% to 5%.
*   **Analyst Sentiment (2023-03-03):** An analyst turned bullish, citing expectations that cost headwinds in categories like Baby Care and Feminine Care will abate in 2023, allowing for share growth momentum and increased investment.
*   **Market Perception (2023-03-23, 2023-03-28):** Recent news articles characterize PG as a defensive "dividend stock" and a "consumer staples titan" that performs well in market downturns and recessions. The stock was noted as being down ~12% from its high but not considered cheap.
*   **Competitive/Strategic Context (2023-03-23):** A news article suggests CPG companies like P&G face challenges in consumer loyalty and differentiation, proposing cannabis as a potential growth avenue.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Environmental & Reputational (2023-03-16):** An investor brief highlights deforestation in P&G's supply chain as a clear risk, urging shareholders to support a proposal for increased reporting.
*   **Risk: Product Safety & Litigation (2023-02-10):** A report notes the finding of benzene in dry shampoos, creating potential liability for P&G if products are deemed to have caused injury.
*   **Offset: Regulatory Compliance (10-K, 2022-08-05):** The company states it has compliance programs and experts to guide adherence to global laws and regulations, and does not expect compliance expenditures to materially impact fiscal 2023 results.
*   **Offset: Sustainability Focus (10-K, 2022-08-05):** Environmental sustainability is described as a key focus integrated into business strategy, with public ambitions to reduce emissions, plastic use, and increase responsible sourcing.

**4. Qualitative Assessment**
**Positive.** The supplied evidence portrays P&G as a resilient, high-quality franchise with a clear strategy for growth and a defensive market position. Recent management guidance for 4-5% organic growth and analyst commentary on easing cost pressures provide a constructive near-term outlook. The company's scale, brand strength, and focus on superiority are consistent with a durable competitive advantage. Key risks related to environmental scrutiny and product liability are present but are partially mitigated by the company's stated compliance and sustainability frameworks. The narrative supports a view of stable, high-quality business execution.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'net margin ranging from 0.179 to 0.185 over the past year' but the raw valuation ratios show net_margin range as 0.2..0.2 (i.e., 0.179 to 0.185 across the sparse history, with the range field showing 0.2 due to rounding). The report's claim is consistent with the sparse history data points (0.179-0.185), so this is acceptable but the quant report should note the range field in the raw data shows 0.2..0.2, which could confuse readers.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; the sparse history confirms the 0.179-0.185 range. Minor clarity note only.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'an analyst turned bullish' citing easing cost headwinds in Baby Care and Feminine Care. The raw evidence (2023-03-03 Benzinga article) confirms this. However, the qual report does not mention the benzene dry shampoo risk (2023-02-10) is sourced from 'injurylawyer.com', a plaintiff-side site, which may overstate the materiality of this risk. The report presents it alongside the NRDC deforestation brief without noting the source bias.
corrections.required_action: PM should note the benzene risk source is a plaintiff-side injury lawyer site, not a regulatory finding or company disclosure, which may overstate near-term litigation probability.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Total equity has decreased from $46.92B (2021-03-31) to $44.73B (2022-12-31), a decline of 4.7%.' The raw balance sheet shows equity was $46.85B at 2022-06-30 and $46.92B at 2021-03-31, so the decline is from the oldest to newest point. However, equity also rose from $44.33B (2022-09-30) to $44.73B (2022-12-31), a QoQ increase. The report's framing of 'gradual erosion' is directionally correct over the full window but omits the recent QoQ stabilization.
corrections.required_action: PM should note equity stabilized QoQ in the most recent quarter (2022-09-30 to 2022-12-31: $44.33B to $44.73B), partially offsetting the longer-term decline narrative.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: PG is a high-quality defensive franchise with stable margins (46.6% gross, 17.9% net) and management-guided 4-5% organic sales growth, but recent earnings momentum is negative (EPS -4.2% YoY) and valuation is middling (P/E 25.7, near the center of its 1-year range). The risk-adjusted outlook is mildly positive but not compelling enough for a stronger conviction call.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: PG is a high-quality defensive franchise with stable margins (46.6% gross, 17.9% net) and management-guided 4-5% organic sales growth, but recent earnings momentum is negative (EPS -4.2% YoY) and valuation is middling (P/E 25.7, near the center of its 1-year range). The risk-adjusted outlook is mildly positive but not compelling enough for a stronger conviction call.
key_factors: Stable high margins: gross margin 46.6%, net margin 17.9-18.5% over the past year, demonstrating durable pricing power and cost control in a challenging input-cost environment.
Negative earnings momentum: TTM EPS declined -4.2% YoY and net income fell -6.7%, reflecting cost headwinds in categories like Baby Care and Feminine Care that analysts expect to ease in 2023.
Valuation is fair but not cheap: P/E of 25.7 sits near the midpoint of its 1-year range (21.3-28.3), and P/B of 8.25 is in the upper half of its range, leaving limited margin of safety.
Management raised FY2023 organic sales growth guidance to 4-5% (January 2023), and analyst sentiment (March 2023) has turned constructive on easing cost pressures, supporting a stabilization thesis.

Balance sheet equity declined 4.7% over the full window but stabilized QoQ in the most recent quarter ($44.33B to $44.73B), and the benzene dry shampoo risk is sourced from a plaintiff-side site rather than regulatory action, tempering its near-term materiality.

NKE.quantitative_report:
**NKE Quantitative Report (Point-in-Time: 2023-04-01)**

**1. Financial Trajectory (Latest: Q2 2022-11-30)**
*   **Revenue & Earnings:** TTM revenue is $49.11B. Latest quarterly revenue was $13.31B, showing a YoY increase of +17.2% but a QoQ increase of +4.9%. Quarterly EPS diluted declined QoQ from $0.93 to $0.85 (-8.6%), with a YoY increase of +2.4%. TTM net income was $5.63B.
*   **Margins:** Latest gross margin was 42.9% ($5.71B / $13.31B), up QoQ from 44.3% but the YoY growth of +9.6% in gross profit lagged revenue growth. Operating margin was 12.4% ($1.65B / $13.31B), down from 14.4% in the prior quarter. Net margin was 11.5% on a TTM basis.
*   **Cash Flow & Balance Sheet:** Cash flow data is unavailable. Total equity was $15.27B. The balance sheet shows total assets of $39.65B. Total debt was reported as $9.42B in the period ending 2022-05-31.

**2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** Weak signal. Despite revenue growth, quarterly net income declined -9.3% QoQ and -0.4% YoY. EPS diluted growth (+2.4% YoY) significantly lagged revenue growth (+17.2% YoY), indicating cost pressures. Net margin contracted from 13.1% (Q1 2022) to 11.5% (TTM).
*   **Balance Sheet Resilience:** Limited assessment without cash flow. Equity base of $15.27B supports asset base of $39.65B. Reported debt of $9.42B (from 2022-05-31) is notable but full liability/equity composition is incomplete in later periods.

**3. Valuation vs. History**
*   The current PE diluted is 34.2x, within its 12-month range of 22.2x to 36.1x. The P/B ratio is 12.6x, also within its range of 8.7x to 14.9x.
*   Valuation context: The current PE of 34.2x is elevated relative to the low of 22.2x (Oct 2022) and near the high of 36.1x. This premium coincides with a period of declining net margin (from 0.133 in April 2022 to 0.115) and stable ROE (0.369 vs. 0.413 a year prior).

**4. Price Level & Returns vs. Fundamentals**
*   **Price:** Closed at $122.64 on 2023-03-31. The 52-week range is $82.22 to $139.14.
*   **Returns:** The stock is down -11.5% over the last year. Quarterly returns are volatile: +4.5% (Q1 2023), +23.1% (Q4 2022), -6.7% (Q3 2022), -26.2% (Q2 2022).
*   **Fundamental Link:** The -11.5% annual price decline occurred alongside TTM revenue growth but flat/down net income and expanding valuation multiples. The sharp quarterly price moves (e.g., -26.2% and +23.1%) show high sensitivity to sentiment shifts, not clearly matched by the smoother, modest fundamental trajectory reported.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside**
*   **Upside:** TTM revenue of $49.11B demonstrates scale and growth. The stock has recovered +23.1% in a single quarter (Q4 2022), showing capacity for sharp re-ratings. Gross profit grew +9.6% YoY.
*   **Downside:** Quarterly EPS diluted has declined QoQ. Net margin is contracting. The current PE of 34.2x is near the top of its range despite flat/down net income (YoY -0.4% QoQ -9.3%), creating valuation risk if earnings do not re-accelerate. The absence of cash flow data prevents a complete cash generation assessment.

**6. Provisional Risk/Reward Conclusion**
The quantitative profile presents a tension: a premium valuation (PE 34.2x) is set against a deteriorating earnings trajectory (declining margins and quarterly net income). Price has recovered meaningfully from 52-week lows but remains below all-time highs, showing sensitivity to earnings momentum. The risk is that valuation compresses toward the lower end of its historical range (e.g., PE of 22x) if earnings weakness persists. Reward is contingent on a fundamental reacceleration in revenue growth translating to bottom-line expansion, which recent quarterly data does not yet support. The balance of evidence suggests valuation is stretched relative to the current earnings trajectory.
NKE.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Analysis: Nike, Inc. (NKE)**
**Report Date:** 2023-04-01 (All evidence strictly point-in-time before this date)

### 1. Business/Franchise Read from Supplied Narrative
Based on the provided text, Nike operates as a global leader in athletic footwear and apparel. The franchise is characterized by strong brand value and a successful pivot towards a Direct-to-Consumer (D2C) and digital-first strategy. Evidence indicates Nike Direct sales, particularly through digital channels, are a primary growth engine. The business demonstrates broad geographic reach, with the notable exception of ongoing challenges in China.

### 2. Recent Developments (Dated)
*   **Financial Performance & Outlook (2023-03-21):** Nike reported quarterly results that beat estimates and subsequently **raised its full-year revenue outlook**. Growth was described as broad-based, led by the Digital direct channel and wholesale revenue. However, the company warned of continued **margin pressures** due to inventory management efforts.
*   **Geographic & Inventory Headwinds (2023-03-21):** The quarter was "plagued by bloated inventory" and weak sales in China, which struggled with COVID-19 lockdowns. Outside of China, Nike saw double-digit sales increases in all other major markets.
*   **D2C Strategy Execution (2023-03-24, 2023-03-22):** Reports emphasize the D2C sales strategy "paying off," with Nike Direct sales and overall revenues rising. The strategy's strength is cited as a core reason for the recent sales beat.
*   **Corporate Action - Litigation (2023-02-14):** Nike filed a trademark infringement lawsuit against Japanese streetwear brand BAPE, alleging violations of its Air Force 1, Jordan 1, and Dunk designs. Nike stated it had no choice but to "act now" as BAPE's infringements had grown to become a "significant danger."

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
*   **Risk: Inventory Overhang & Margin Pressure:** The narrative from the Q3 report explicitly highlights "bloated inventory" and "margin pressures" as current operational challenges.
    *   *Offset:* The company raised its full-year revenue outlook, indicating management's confidence in navigating the inventory cycle through strong demand.
*   **Risk: China Market Volatility:** Sales in China are repeatedly cited as "weak" and a struggle due to external factors (COVID lockdowns), presenting a geographic concentration risk.
    *   *Offset:* Performance outside China was consistently strong, with "double-digit sales increases in all of its other markets."
*   **Risk: Intellectual Property & Litigation:** The 2023-02-14 lawsuit and subsequent industry commentary (2023-03-14, 2023-03-01) indicate Nike is intensifying its fight against design infringement. This exposes the company to legal costs and potential industry friction.
    *   *Offset:* Proactive litigation is a defense of Nike's most valuable asset—its trademarks and designs—which are core to its brand premium.

### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Cautiously Positive.**
The confirmed narrative shows Nike executing on its strategic priorities, achieving strong top-line growth outside of China, and successfully leveraging its D2C model to beat expectations and raise its revenue outlook. These are clear positives for the franchise. However, the acknowledged inventory and margin headwinds, coupled with persistent China weakness, introduce near-term operational uncertainty. The raised outlook is a positive signal, but the path to improving profitability faces tangible headwinds. The assessment is therefore positive but tempered by the cited operational risks.

***
*Evidence Confined to Supplied Text:* All developments and risks are drawn directly from the provided company news, web context snippets, and cited dates (2023-02-14, 2023-03-21, 2023-03-22, 2023-03-24). No financial metrics or future projections are invented.*

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Net margin contracted from 13.1% (Q1 2022) to 11.5% (TTM)' — the 13.1% figure corresponds to the net_margin=0.131 reported in the valuation sparse history for 2022-05-05 through 2022-06-07, but labeling it 'Q1 2022' is ambiguous. The raw income statement shows Q1 2022 (2022-08-31) had net_income=1.47B on revenue=12.69B, which is 11.6%, not 13.1%. The 13.1% appears to be a trailing metric from the valuation panel, not a quarterly margin. The report should clarify whether it is citing a trailing net margin or a quarterly margin to avoid misleading the reader.
corrections.required_action: Clarify that the 13.1% is a trailing net margin from the valuation panel (as of ~May 2022), not the Q1 2022 quarterly net margin (which was ~11.6%). This distinction matters because the quarterly margin was already lower than the trailing figure.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'ROE (0.369 vs. 0.413 a year prior)' — the raw valuation data shows ROE=0.369 at 2023-03-31 and ROE=0.413 at 2022-04-01, which is roughly a year prior. However, the report describes ROE as 'stable' while also noting it declined from 0.413 to 0.369, a ~440bp drop. A ~10% decline in ROE is not consistent with the characterization 'stable.'
corrections.required_action: Revise the ROE characterization from 'stable' to 'declining' to match the reported 0.413→0.369 drop.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'Nike Direct sales, particularly through digital channels, are a primary growth engine' and cites 'Digital direct channel (25% YoY growth)' from the Ada Insights source dated 2023-03-24. The raw web context for that source confirms the 25% figure. However, the qual report also states 'Nike Direct sales rose 17% to $5.3 billion' citing the 2023-03-25 Fool source, and separately 'digital sales were up 20%.' The 25% and 20% digital growth figures come from different sources and may reflect different definitions or time periods. The report does not reconcile these, which could overstate the precision of the digital growth narrative.

unsupported_claims: : The quant report's claim that 'Net margin contracted from 13.1% (Q1 2022) to 11.5% (TTM)' conflates a trailing valuation-panel net margin (0.131 as of ~May 2022) with a label of 'Q1 2022.' The raw quarterly income statement for Q1 2022 (2022-08-31) shows net income of $1.47B on $12.69B revenue = 11.6% quarterly margin, not 13.1%. The 13.1% is not a quarterly figure from the income statement pack.

**Recommendation**: KEEP
**Severity**: minor

**Verified points:**
- (none)

**Required corrections:**
- [C1] Quant report states 'Net margin contracted from 13.1% (Q1 2022) to 11.5% (TTM)' — the 13.1% figure corresponds to the net_margin=0.131 reported in the valuation sparse history for 2022-05-05 through 2022-06-07, but labeling it 'Q1 2022' is ambiguous. The raw income statement shows Q1 2022 (2022-08-31) had net_income=1.47B on revenue=12.69B, which is 11.6%, not 13.1%. The 13.1% appears to be a trailing metric from the valuation panel, not a quarterly margin. The report should clarify whether it is citing a trailing net margin or a quarterly margin to avoid misleading the reader. → Clarify that the 13.1% is a trailing net margin from the valuation panel (as of ~May 2022), not the Q1 2022 quarterly net margin (which was ~11.6%). This distinction matters because the quarterly margin was already lower than the trailing figure.
- [C2] Quant report states 'ROE (0.369 vs. 0.413 a year prior)' — the raw valuation data shows ROE=0.369 at 2023-03-31 and ROE=0.413 at 2022-04-01, which is roughly a year prior. However, the report describes ROE as 'stable' while also noting it declined from 0.413 to 0.369, a ~440bp drop. A ~10% decline in ROE is not consistent with the characterization 'stable.' → Revise the ROE characterization from 'stable' to 'declining' to match the reported 0.413→0.369 drop.
- [C3] Qual report states 'Nike Direct sales, particularly through digital channels, are a primary growth engine' and cites 'Digital direct channel (25% YoY growth)' from the Ada Insights source dated 2023-03-24. The raw web context for that source confirms the 25% figure. However, the qual report also states 'Nike Direct sales rose 17% to $5.3 billion' citing the 2023-03-25 Fool source, and separately 'digital sales were up 20%.' The 25% and 20% digital growth figures come from different sources and may reflect different definitions or time periods. The report does not reconcile these, which could overstate the precision of the digital growth narrative. → Note that the 25% and 20% digital growth figures come from different sources and may not be directly comparable; avoid presenting them as a single confirmed data point.

**Unsupported claims:**
- : The quant report's claim that 'Net margin contracted from 13.1% (Q1 2022) to 11.5% (TTM)' conflates a trailing valuation-panel net margin (0.131 as of ~May 2022) with a label of 'Q1 2022.' The raw quarterly income statement for Q1 2022 (2022-08-31) shows net income of $1.47B on $12.69B revenue = 11.6% quarterly margin, not 13.1%. The 13.1% is not a quarterly figure from the income statement pack.
- : The quant report characterizes ROE as 'stable' while simultaneously reporting a decline from 0.413 to 0.369 (~11% drop). This is internally inconsistent — the raw data does not support 'stable' as a descriptor for a 440bp decline.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Nike presents a classic quality-at-a-premium-valuation dilemma: the brand and D2C strategy are executing well with strong top-line growth and a raised revenue outlook, but a PE of 34.2x is set against declining margins, contracting net income, and a ~10% ROE decline. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Nike presents a classic quality-at-a-premium-valuation dilemma: the brand and D2C strategy are executing well with strong top-line growth and a raised revenue outlook, but a PE of 34.2x is set against declining margins, contracting net income, and a ~10% ROE decline. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels.
key_factors: Premium valuation stretched: PE of 34.2x sits near the top of its 12-month range (22.2x–36.1x) despite quarterly net income declining -9.3% QoQ and -0.4% YoY, and net margin contracting from ~13.1% trailing (May 2022) to 11.5% TTM.

D2C and digital strategy driving top-line: Nike raised its full-year revenue outlook (2023-03-21) after beating estimates, with digital channel growth of 20-25% YoY and Nike Direct sales rising 17% to $5.3B, demonstrating successful execution of the D2C pivot.

China weakness and inventory overhang: Q3 results were 'plagued by bloated inventory' and weak China sales due to COVID lockdowns, creating near-term margin pressure even as ex-China markets delivered double-digit growth.

ROE declining meaningfully: ROE fell from 0.413 (April 2022) to 0.369 (March 2023), a ~440bp drop, contradicting any narrative of stable profitability and signaling that revenue growth is not translating efficiently to shareholder returns.

Item 1. Business.
Overview
JPMorgan Chase & Co. ("JPMorgan Chase" or the "Firm", NYSE: JPM), a financial holding company incorporated under Delaware law in 1968, is a leading financial services firm based in the United States of America ("U.S."), with operations worldwide. JPMorgan Chase had $3.7 trillion in assets and $292.3 billion in stockholders' equity as of December 31, 2022. The Firm is a leader in investment banking, financial services for consumers and small businesses, commercial banking, financial transaction processing and asset management. Under the J.P. Morgan and Chase brands, the Firm serves millions of customers, predominantly in the U.S., and many of the world's most prominent corporate, institutional and government clients globally.
JPMorgan Chase's principal bank subsidiary is JPMorgan Chase Bank, National Association ("JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A."), a national banking association with U.S. branches in 48 states and Washington, D.C. JPMorgan Chase's principal non-bank subsidiary is J.P. Morgan Securities LLC ("J.P. Morgan Securities"), a U.S. broker-dealer. The bank and non-bank subsidiaries of JPMorgan Chase operate nationally as well as through overseas branches and subsidiaries, representative offices and subsidiary foreign banks. The Firm's principal operating subsidiaries outside the U.S. are J.P. Morgan Securities plc and J.P. Morgan SE ("JPMSE"), which are subsidiaries of JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. and are based in the United Kingdom ("U.K.") and Germany, respectively.
The Firm's website is www.jpmorganchase.com. JPMorgan Chase makes available on its website, free of charge, annual reports on Form 10-K, quarterly reports on Form 10-Q and current reports on Form 8-K pursuant to Section 13(a) or Section 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as soon as reasonably practicable after it electronically files or furnishes such material to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC") at www.sec.gov. JPMorgan Chase makes new and important information about the Firm available on its website at https://www.jpmorganchase.com, including on the Investor Relations section of its website at https://www.jpmorganchase.com/ir. Information on the Firm's website is not incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2022 ("2022 Form 10-K" or "Form 10-K") or the Firm's other filings with the SEC. The Firm has adopted, and posted on its website, a Code of Conduct for all employees of the Firm and a Code of Ethics for its Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, Chief Financial Officer, Principal Accounting Officer and all other professionals of the Firm worldwide serving in a finance, accounting, treasury, tax or investor relations role. The Code of Ethics is also available in print upon request to the Firm's Investor Relations team. Within the time period required by the SEC, JPMorgan Chase will post on its website any amendment to the Code of Ethics and any waiver applicable to a director or executive officer.
Business segments
For management reporting purposes, JPMorgan Chase's activities are organized into four major reportable business segments, as well as a Corporate segment. The Firm's consumer business is the Consumer & Community Banking ("CCB") segment. The Firm's wholesale business segments are the Corporate & Investment Bank ("CIB"), Commercial Banking ("CB"), and Asset & Wealth Management ("AWM").
A description of the Firm's business segments and the products and services they provide to their respective client bases is provided in the "Business segment results" section of Management's discussion and analysis of financial condition and results of operations ("Management's discussion and analysis" or "MD&A"), beginning on page 46 and in Note 32.
Competition
JPMorgan Chase and its subsidiaries and affiliates operate in highly competitive environments. Competitors include other banks, brokerage firms, investment banking companies, merchant banks, hedge funds, commodity trading companies, private equity firms, insurance companies, mutual fund companies, investment managers, credit card companies, mortgage banking companies, trust companies, securities processing companies, automobile financing companies, leasing companies, e-commerce and other internet-based companies, financial technology companies, and other companies engaged in providing similar as well as new products and services. The Firm's businesses generally compete on the basis of the quality and variety of the Firm's products and services, transaction execution, innovation, reputation and price. Competition also varies based on the types of clients, customers, industries and geographies served. With respect to some of its geographies and products, JPMorgan Chase competes globally; with respect to others, the Firm competes on a national or regional basis. New competitors in the financial services industry continue to emerge, including firms that offer products and services solely through the internet and non-financial companies that offer products and services that disintermediate traditional banking products and services offered by financial services firms such as JPMorgan Chase.

JPM.quantitative_report:
## JPM — Quantitative Report (Decision Date: 2023-04-01)

---

### 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue** bottomed at Q4-2021 ($29.26B) and accelerated for four consecutive quarters to Q4-2022 ($34.55B), delivering QoQ growth of +5.6% and YoY growth of +18.1%. TTM revenue stands at $128.69B.

**Earnings (EPS diluted)** hit a trough of $2.63 in Q1-2022, then recovered sequentially to $2.76 → $3.12 → $3.58 across Q2–Q4 2022. YoY Q4 EPS growth was +7.2%, substantially lagging revenue growth. TTM net income is $37.68B. However, Q4-2022 EPS ($3.58) remains well below the cycle peak of $4.50 recorded in Q1-2021 (–20.4% from peak).

**Margins** have compressed materially. TTM operating margin is 35.9% (operating income $46.17B / revenue $128.69B). TTM net margin is 29.3%, situated at the bottom of the one-year observed range (0.30–0.40). Net margin has declined from approximately 40% in early 2022 to 29.3% currently, even as revenue has grown.

**Cash flow:** No quarterly cash-flow data is available. Assessment of free-cash-flow generation is not possible from the supplied evidence.

**Balance sheet:** Total assets have contracted from a peak of $3,954.69B (Q1-2022) to $3,665.74B (Q4-2022), a –7.3% decline, consistent with balance-sheet runoff. Total equity of $292.33B is slightly above the mid-2022 low ($285.90B, Q1-2022) but below the Q4-2021 level of $294.13B. The equity-to-assets ratio is 7.97%. Leverage (liabilities/equity) is 11.54x, within normal bank parameters.

---

### 2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality — margin divergence from revenue growth:** Q4-2022 YoY revenue growth of +18.1% yielded only +7.2% EPS growth and +5.9% net-income growth. This gap signals significant cost or provision growth that is diluting the bottom line's sensitivity to top-line gains. Net margin has compressed from ~40% to ~29.3% over the observation window — a roughly 10–11 percentage-point contraction.

**ROE deterioration:** ROE has declined from 16.4% (April 2022) to 12.9% (March 2023), sitting at the floor of the one-year range (0.10–0.20). This coincides with both margin compression and a relatively flat equity base, indicating declining capital efficiency.

**Balance-sheet resilience:** Total equity increased sequentially from $288.02B (Q3-2022) to $292.33B (Q4-2022), a modest +1.5% improvement. However, equity is essentially flat over the eight-quarter window ($294.13B → $292.33B), meaning retained earnings have been largely offset by capital returns or other adjustments. Asset contraction reduces risk-weighted exposure but also limits balance-sheet-driven revenue capacity.

**Cash-flow quality:** Unassessable. Free-cash-flow and operating-cash-flow data are unavailable.

---

### 3. Valuation Versus Company's Own History

| Metric | Current (2023-03-31) | 1-Year Range | Position in Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E diluted | 10.778 | 7.6–11.9 | **88th percentile** |
| P/B | 1.324 | 1.1–1.5 | **56th percentile** |
| P/S | 3.007 | — | — |
| Earnings yield | 9.3% | — | — |
| Net margin | 0.293 | 0.30–0.40 | **Floor** |
| ROE | 0.129 | 0.10–0.20 | **Floor** |

The P/E multiple has expanded from 7.6x (July 2022) to 10.8x today, placing it near the top of the one-year range. The earnings yield of 9.3% appears attractive in absolute terms, but this must be weighed against the fact that net margin and ROE are both at their lowest observed levels — the "E" in the P/E is being generated at trough profitability, not at normalized or peak levels. P/B at 1.32x is near the midpoint of its range and reflects the flat equity base.

**Key valuation tension:** The multiple has expanded to near-range highs while underlying earnings quality metrics (margin, ROE) sit at range floors. The stock is not cheap relative to its own history if margins remain depressed; the low P/E partly reflects cyclical trough earnings rather than mispricing.

---

### 4. Price Level and Return Context Versus Fundamentals

| Price Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Close (2023-03-31) | $130.31 |
| 52-week high | $144.34 |
| 52-week low | $101.28 |
| Quarterly return (≈63d) | –2.2% |
| Annual return (≈252d) | –7.3% |

The stock rallied +25.5% from Q3-2022 end ($106.16) to Q4-2022 end ($133.22), then gave back –2.2% in Q1-2023. Over the full year, the stock declined –7.3% from $140.54 to $130.31.

**Fundamental-price linkage:** The annual –7.3% price decline occurred alongside +18.1% YoY revenue growth in the latest quarter, but this was offset by severe margin compression (net margin from ~40% to ~29.3%) and ROE contraction (from 16.4% to 12.9%). The market's re-rating to a higher P/E multiple (from ~8.8x to ~10.8x) accompanied the Q4-2022 price recovery, yet the stock remains –9.7% below the 52-week high of $144.34. The current price sits roughly in the middle of the 52-week range ($101.28–$144.34).

---

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside

**Strongest quantitative upside:**
- Revenue momentum is unambiguously positive: four consecutive quarters of sequential growth with Q4-2022 YoY growth of +18.1%. If this trajectory continues and margins stabilize or recover, EPS could approach prior-cycle peaks ($4.50 per share in Q1-2021). At the current P/E of 10.8x, that would imply a price of ~$48.60 per quarter or a TTM EPS run-rate approaching $14–$15, implying ~$150–$160 on a maintained multiple.
- Earnings yield of 9.3% provides a high income baseline relative to broad-market alternatives.
- P/B at 1.32x remains below the observed ceiling of 1.5x, leaving room for re-rating if ROE recovers.

**Strongest quantitative downside:**
- The P/E is at the 88th percentile of its range while both net margin (29.3%) and ROE (12.9%) are at floor levels. If margins compress further or fail to recover, the multiple will likely contract toward the lower end of the range (7.6x), implying a potential price of ~$76–$85 on current TTM EPS of ~$10–$11.
- Revenue growth (+18.1% YoY) is decoupling from earnings growth (+7.2% YoY), indicating operating leverage is working in reverse — cost or credit provisions are absorbing incremental revenue.
- The Q1-2023 quarterly return of –2.2% follows the large Q4-2022 rally of +25.5%, suggesting momentum exhaustion near the current level.
- No cash-flow data to confirm whether reported earnings are converting to cash.

---

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

**Risk/reward is balanced to modestly unfavorable at current levels.** The stock trades at a P/E of 10.78x, near the top of its one-year range (7.6–11.9), while the underlying earnings engine is producing at its lowest observed margin (29.3%) and ROE (12.9%) levels. The +18.1% revenue growth is a genuine positive, but only +7.2% of that is flowing to EPS, and absolute earnings remain 20.4% below cycle-peak levels ($3.58 vs. $4.50).

**Upside case** requires margin stabilization and a sustained revenue trajectory, which could push TTM EPS toward $14–$15 and support prices of $150–$160 on a stable ~10–11x multiple — roughly +15–23% from the current $130.31.

**Downside case** involves further margin erosion or multiple contraction toward the range floor (7.6x), which on depressed earnings could yield prices in the $76–$101 area — representing –23% to –42% from current levels.

The asymmetric skew — with the P/E at 88% of its range and profitability metrics at floor levels — suggests that the burden of proof rests on margin recovery to justify the current valuation. Without cash-flow data to corroborate earnings quality, the quantitative case for re-rating from here is unsupported by the available evidence. The base-case return appears bounded by the tension between strong revenue momentum (upside) and near-trough profitability multiples already priced in (limited further re-rating).
JPM.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM)**  
**Decision Date:** 2023-04-01  

### 1. Business/Franchise Read  
Based on the 10-K filed on 2023-02-21, JPM is a leading U.S. financial services firm with $3.7 trillion in assets and $292.3 billion in stockholders' equity (as of 2022-12-31). It operates globally across four main segments: Consumer & Community Banking (CCB), Corporate & Investment Bank (CIB), Commercial Banking (CB), and Asset & Wealth Management (AWM). The firm competes in highly competitive markets against banks, fintech, and non-financial companies, with competition based on product quality, innovation, and price. Its principal subsidiaries include JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. (with branches in 48 U.S. states) and J.P. Morgan Securities LLC.

### 2. Recent Developments (Pre-2023-04-01)  
- **Sector Sentiment (2023-03-31):** Bank stocks ended Q1 2023 with gains but faced double-digit YTD losses due to liquidity concerns and outflows from savings accounts.  
- **Earnings Outlook (2023-03-31):** The Zacks Finance sector was expected to report +2.2% higher earnings and +7% higher revenues for Q1 2023. JPM was highlighted among peers.  
- **Stock Performance (2023-03-29):** JPM closed at $129.14, gaining +0.2% but lagging the broader market.  
- **Upcoming Earnings (2023-03-23):** JPM’s next earnings release was anticipated around 2023-04-14, with analysts expecting earnings reports.  
- **Long-Term Catalysts (2023-02-24):** Morgan Stanley analysts projected JPM’s market capitalization could reach $1 trillion by 2030, reinforcing its position as the most valuable U.S. bank.  
- **Economic Concerns (2023-02-24):** JPM stated its 2023 net interest income would be lower than analysts expected due to signs of economic slippage.  

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets  
**Risks:**  
- **Legal/Reputational Risk:** On 2023-03-21 and 2023-03-22, a U.S. judge ordered JPM to face lawsuits alleging it enabled Jeffrey Epstein’s sex trafficking, potentially exposing the bank to financial and reputational damage.  
- **Operational Risk (2023-03-22):** JPM was involved in a commodities fraud case related to alleged nickel fraud, indicating vulnerabilities in operational controls.  
- **Economic Sensitivity (2023-02-24):** Lower-than-expected net interest income guidance reflected concerns about economic weakness affecting lending profitability.  
- **Competitive Pressure (10-K, 2023-02-21):** The firm faces intense competition from traditional and emerging players (e.g., fintech), which could pressure margins and market share.  

**Offsets:**  
- **Scale and Diversification (10-K, 2023-02-21):** JPM’s vast asset base, diversified business segments, and global operations provide resilience against localized risks.  
- **Market Leadership (10-K, 2023-02-21):** The firm holds leading positions in investment banking, consumer banking, and financial transaction processing.  
- **Long-Term Growth Potential (2023-02-24):** Analyst projections of a $1 trillion market cap by 2030 suggest confidence in sustained franchise value.  

### 4. Qualitative Assessment  
**Neutral.**  
The evidence presents a balanced picture: JPM’s dominant franchise and long-term growth catalysts are counterbalanced by near-term legal overhangs, economic headwinds, and competitive pressures. While the firm’s structural strengths provide a solid foundation, unresolved lawsuits and macroeconomic uncertainties introduce material qualitative risks that temper optimism. Recent developments reflect stabilization in sector sentiment but no definitive positive inflection.  

*Note: All assessments are based solely on the point-in-time evidence provided; no financial data or projections beyond the supplied text are inferred.*

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Net margin has declined from approximately 40% in early 2022 to 29.3% currently.' The raw evidence shows net_margin=0.397 on 2022-04-01 and 0.293 on 2023-03-31, confirming the ~40% to ~29.3% trajectory. However, the report also says the one-year observed range is 0.30–0.40, which is slightly inconsistent — the floor is 0.293 (current), not 0.30. This is a minor rounding issue but could mislead on whether the current level is at or below the stated range floor.
corrections.required_action: Clarify that the net margin floor is 0.293 (current reading), not 0.30, to avoid ambiguity about whether the metric has broken below its stated range.

corrections.issue: Quant report's upside scenario states: 'EPS could approach prior-cycle peaks ($4.50 per share in Q1-2021). At the current P/E of 10.8x, that would imply a price of ~$48.60 per quarter or a TTM EPS run-rate approaching $14–$15, implying ~$150–$160 on a maintained multiple.' The $48.60 figure is arithmetically unclear (10.8 × $4.50 = $48.60, but this is a single-quarter price, not a stock price). The report then jumps to TTM EPS of $14–$15, which would require all four quarters to hit ~$3.50–$3.75, not the $4.50 peak. The reasoning conflates a single-quarter peak with a TTM run-rate in a confusing way.
corrections.required_action: Clarify the upside math: if all four quarters matched the Q1-2021 peak of $4.50, TTM EPS would be $18.00, not $14–$15. If the intent is a more conservative recovery to ~$3.50–$3.75/quarter, state that explicitly. The current presentation is internally inconsistent.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'JPM stated its 2023 net interest income would be lower than analysts expected due to signs of economic slippage.' The raw evidence confirms this from the 2023-02-24 article. However, the qual report does not quantify or contextualize the magnitude of the NII guidance cut, which is a material near-term earnings headwind that aligns with the quant report's margin compression thesis. This omission weakens the linkage between the two reports.
corrections.required_action: No action required for scoring, but the PM should note that the NII guidance cut is a confirmed qualitative headwind that reinforces the quant report's margin concern.

unsupported_claims: Quant report states 'ROE has declined from 16.4% (April 2022) to 12.9% (March 2023), sitting at the floor of the one-year range (0.10–0.20).' The raw evidence confirms roe=0.164 on 2022-04-01 and roe=0.129 on 2023-03-31, and the range is 0.10–0.20. This is supported.

Quant report states 'The stock rallied +25.5% from Q3-2022 end ($106.16) to Q4-2022 end ($133.22).' Raw evidence shows 2022-09-29 close=106.16 and 2022-12-29 close=133.22, with quarterly delta of +25.5%. This is supported.

Qual report references 'commodities fraud case related to alleged nickel fraud' on 2023-03-22. Raw evidence confirms the 2023-03-22 Business Insider article about JPMorgan's $1.3 million nickel situation. Supported.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: JPM's strong revenue momentum (+18.1% YoY in Q4-2022) and dominant franchise are offset by severe margin compression (net margin at 29.3%, a one-year low) and ROE deterioration (12.9%, also at floor), while the P/E has expanded to the 88th percentile of its range. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels, with the burden of proof on margin recovery to justify further re-rating.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: JPM's strong revenue momentum (+18.1% YoY in Q4-2022) and dominant franchise are offset by severe margin compression (net margin at 29.3%, a one-year low) and ROE deterioration (12.9%, also at floor), while the P/E has expanded to the 88th percentile of its range. The risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels, with the burden of proof on margin recovery to justify further re-rating.
key_factors: Revenue growth of +18.1% YoY in Q4-2022 with four consecutive quarters of sequential expansion, yet EPS grew only +7.2% YoY, indicating significant cost or provision absorption of incremental revenue.

P/E of 10.78x sits at the 88th percentile of its one-year range (7.6–11.9) while net margin (29.3%) and ROE (12.9%) are both at their one-year floor levels — the stock is priced for recovery that has not yet materialized in profitability metrics.
JPM's 2023 net interest income guidance was cut below analyst expectations (2023-02-24), reinforcing the margin compression thesis and signaling near-term earnings headwinds from economic softness.

Legal overhang from Epstein-related lawsuits (2023-03-21/22) and operational risk from the nickel fraud case (2023-03-22) introduce reputational and financial uncertainty that tempers the qualitative case.
